back: The time immediately following a stimulus when depolarization prevents another response of the muscle cell from occurring.
That portion of the swing in which momentum is increased and the non-weight-bearing limb has propulsion forward. Also known as early swing.
A hereditary condition signified by short limbs due to ossification of long bone cartilage.
The second ground reaction force peak. The peak occurs during the last half of support and is usually less than the impact peak.
A brief nerve impulse created from a rapid change in the electrical membrane potential on a nerve or muscle cell’s surface to cause a nerve to reverse its polarity or a muscle cell to contract.

A condition that generally occurs in the glenohumeral joint. Inflammation within the joint’s capsule causes capsular wall thickening and adherence. Loss of motion in the joint results. Also known as frozen shoulder.

A sensory nerve fiber from the muscle spindle and Golgi tendon organ. There are two primary types, A and C fibers. C fibers are pain receptors; A fibers are tension and length receptors. Group III delta are A receptors that respond to pressure pain. Group I are A receptors: Group Ia form muscle spindle primary endings and Group Ib form Golgi tendon organs. Group II are A receptors and form muscle spindle secondary endings.
A muscle acting as a prime mover to produce a motion.
Occurs when a person inhales infected droplets that have become airborne (i.e., exposure to bacteria or virus through coughing and sneezing).
A graft taken from one person and transplanted into another.

General term used to describe various changes in a patient’s mental functioning that can range from slight confusion to coma. These can include signs and symptoms such as confusion, amnesia, disorientation, and unusual or strange behavior.

Atraumatic, multidirectional, bilateral, rehabilitation effective, inferior capsular shift required. A condition of nontraumatic shoulder instability that is multidirectional but usually responds well to the conservative treatment of rehabilitation.
The second phase of a plyometric activity that is the rapid transition from eccentric to concentric motion.
The space at the base of the thumb created by the extensor pollicis longus and brevis tendons.
Formation of new blood vessels.
A muscle that opposes the motion of another muscle.
A painful or abnormal walking or running pattern.
The front or top surface of a limb.

A ligament crossing through the knee joint that attaches from the anterior tibia to the posterior femur. The anterior cruciate ligament limits anterior movement of the tibia from the femur as well as rotation of the tibia.
Loose connective tissue with an unorganized structure and relatively long distances between cross-links.

Reflex inhibition of muscle activity following injury. It is thought to be a protective mechanism for the joint to reduce excessive stresses that could be applied by strong muscles surrounding the joint. Most often, AMI is discussed as it relates to quadriceps inhibition following knee joint injury.
The motions between the bones that make up a joint, including roll, slide, spin, compression, and distraction.

A protective mechanism provided by the Golgi tendon organ, in which a Golgi tendon organ stimulus, facilitated by a sudden stretch, causes a reflex activation of the antagonist and relaxation of the agonist.
A graft of articular cartilage and bone plug that is taken from the same joint as the damaged cartilage, but it is removed from a part of the joint that does not bear weight.
The tearing away of a tendon or ligament attachment from bone.
The tearing of the capsulolabral complex from the glenoid rim.
The colloquial term for an avulsion of the extensor digitorum tendon from the distal phalanx of the finger; also known as mallet finger.
A fracture of the first metacarpal.
Involuntary eyelid closure.
Repetitive movements such as hand waving, rocking, or finger flicking. There is no inherent harm in displaying blindisms other than the social stigma.

Pathogenic microorganisms that are present in human blood and can cause disease in humans. These pathogens include, but are not limited to, hepatitis B virus (HBV) and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV).
Deformity characterized by flexion of the PIP joint and hyperextension of the DIP joint.
When the pulley system holding the tendon in place ruptures, so the tendon is no longer in proximity to the bone and displays an appearance similar to that of an archer’s bowstring.
Fracture of the metacarpals secondary to a compressive force.

A local tissue hormone that is activated by the interaction of proteases upon the Hageman factor. A very potent local vasodilator. It increases vascular permeability and stimulates local pain receptors.
Gait tempo measured in steps per minute.

Chemotactic cytokines that are secreted by a number of cells in a wound, such as endothelium, fibroblasts, keratinocytes, neutrophils, and macrophages. They are primarily involved in preventing angiogenesis during clot formation and remodeling phases, but promoting angiogenesis during inflammation and proliferation phases.
Movement of cells or chemicals in response to a chemical stimulus. Vital activities in wound healing that occur through complex and not totally understood processes.
Toes that are extended at the metatarsophalangeal joints and flexed at the proximal and distal interphalangeal joints.
An abnormal response during muscle testing that is observed as a series of catch-and-release tensions rather than a smooth resistance, indicative of the person providing a less-than-maximal effort.

Major structure of the body’s protein. There are five types in the body: Type I, the most abundant type, is high in tensile strength and is found in dermis, fascia, and bone; Type II is found in cartilage; Type III is found in embryonic connective tissue; Types IV and V are found in basement membranes. Forms inelastic bundles to provide structure, integrity, and tensile strength to tissues.
A fracture to the distal radius resulting from a fall on the outstretched hand with the wrist in a hyperextended position.

front: Commission on Accreditation of Athletic Training Education (CAATE)
back: The accrediting agency for athletic training programs.

Sudden arrhythmic death caused by an impact to the chest wall. It is mostly seen in athletes between the ages of 8 and 18 who play sports with projectiles, such as baseballs, hockey pucks, or lacrosse balls. The timing and location of the impact are important factors in determining whether an athlete will have this condition. Immediate CPR and defibrillation are imperative for preventing death; even with CPR and AED use, survival is rare.
A significant rise in intracompartmental pressure caused by severe bleeding within a muscular compartment that can compromise neurovascular structures.
Roll and slide occur in the same direction when a concave surface moves on a convex surface. Also called the concave–convex rule.
Roll and slide occur in opposite directions when a convex surface moves on a concave surface. Also called the convex–concave rule.

Movement of the sacrum when the superior base moves posteriorly as the distal sacrum and coccyx move anteriorly. During counternutation, the iliac crests move apart, and the ischial tuberosities move closer together. Opposite of nutation.
Medical term for snapping hip.
Femoral neck angulation greater than 135°.
Femoral neck angulation less than 120°.
A cracking or grating sound or sensation caused by inflammation or degenerative changes.
An enzyme produced by newly formed epithelial cells and fibroblasts.

Abnormal posturing in which a person is stiff with flexed arms, clenched fists, and legs held out straight. The arms are flexed in toward the body, and the wrists and fingers are bent and held on the chest.
Tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons and their sheaths on the radial side of the thumb.
A condition in which an injury at one site produces signs and symptoms at another site.
The non-support phase in a running stride.
The point of the gait cycle that occurs at the beginning of the stance phase during heel strike for one leg and the end of the stance phase just before toe-off for the other.
The legal obligation to provide care once a professional relationship has been established.
The first part of the swing phase of gait.

An essential protein of connective-tissue elastic structures. Arranged in a wavy orientation. The wavy arrangement allows tissue to change with stress and to resume normal conditions after stress removal. It plays an unknown role in the remodeling phase.
A test for injury to the supraspinatus muscle when the arm is abducted to 90° and placed in the scapular plane while downward resistance is applied.
An overuse injury of the elbow that is displayed with signs and symptoms that include pain over the epicondyle with gripping tasks. See also epicondylopathy.

An overuse injury to the tendinous attachments of the flexor/pronator group at the medial epicondyle or the extensor/supinator group at the lateral epicondyle. Also referred to as tennis elbow when it occurs on the lateral epicondyle and as golfer’s elbow when it occurs on the medial epicondyle.
Inflammation of an epicondyle.

A hormone. Also called adrenaline. A potent stimulator of the sympathetic nervous system. It is also a powerful vasopressor, increasing blood pressure, stimulating the heart muscle, accelerating the heart rate, and increasing cardiac output. It also increases metabolic activities such as glycogenolysis and glucose release.
A medical injury or condition where the epiphysis loosens or separates from the bone shaft.
A life-threatening condition characterized by central nervous system dysfunction and core temperature above 40 °C.
Abnormal bone growth.
The basic material from which tissue develops. Produced by fibroblasts in wounds. Composed of fibers and ground substance. Serves as a foundation on which anything is cast.
External factors such as environment, equipment, or training load that influence injury risk.
A line from the tip of the medial malleolus to the first metatarsal head. It is used as a point of reference to determine the relative position of the navicular tubercle.
Insoluble fibrous protein. Formed by fibrinogen. Important in clotting.
A connective-tissue cell. Fibroblasts differentiate into chondroblasts, osteoblasts, and collagenoblasts. They form the fibrous tissues to support and bind a variety of the body’s tissues.

An eversion deformity of the midtarsal joint causing the medial forefoot to be lower than the lateral forefoot in non–weight bearing when viewed in the same plane as a perpendicular line bisecting the calcaneus.

An inversion deformity of the midtarsal joint causing the medial forefoot to be higher than the lateral forefoot in non–weight bearing when viewed in the same plane as a perpendicular line bisecting the calcaneus.
A term that refers to the stability of the pelvic ring that is provided by the structure and shape of the joints, their ligaments and bony components.
A condition in which the patellae face outward in relation to each other rather than forward.
Excessive knee hyperextension.
Knee alignment in which the knees are angled toward each other. Also called genu valgum.
Knee alignment in which the knees are bowed outward. Also called genu varus.
A standardized neurological scale used to assess the level of consciousness following a head injury.

These afferent receptors are located in joint capsules. They are stimulated by joint compression but not by joint motion. Any weight-bearing activity stimulates these slowly adapting receptors. They are not believed to play a role in proprioception except in the identification of joint compression.
A stretch receptor found in a series within the musculotendinous structure. It responds to muscle contraction more than to muscle stretch to signal force.

Newly formed vascular tissue that is produced during wound healing. Consists of fibroblasts, macrophages, and a neovascular structure within a base of connective-tissue matrix of collagen, hyaluronic acid, and fibronectin. It has the velvety appearance of small, red, nodular masses seen in new tissue. It eventually forms the cicatrix of the wound.
A condition also known as a bunion. Present when the first metatarsophalangeal joint is greater than 10° in valgus, so that the first toe points laterally toward the other toes.
A condition in which the toe is extended at the metatarsophalangeal joint, flexed at the proximal interphalangeal joint, and extended at the distal interphalangeal joint.
The portion of the stance phase of the gait cycle in which the weight begins to transfer to the front of the foot and the heel is lifted off the floor. Also known as the terminal stance.
The portion of the stance phase of the gait cycle in which the heel first comes in contact with the floor. Also known as initial contact.
Vomiting of blood.
A joint replacement surgery in which only half of the joint is replaced.
A benign process characterized by heterotopic ossification, or bone formation, within large muscles.
A local tissue hormone released by mast cells and granulocytes. Increases vascular permeability to proteins and fibronectin.
A law stating that the stress applied to a body to deform it is proportional to the strain as long as the body’s elasticity limit is not exceeded.

A major component of early granulation tissue. Greatest amounts are seen in a wound during the first 4 to 5 days. Promotes cell movement and migration during repair. Stimulates fibroblast proliferation. Produces edema by absorbing large amounts of water to increase fibroblast migration.
An abnormally low blood glucose level that can impair neurological and physical function.
Core temperature below 35 °C (95 °F).
The process of tissue lengthening that results when the tissue cannot withstand forces that are progressively applied to it.
The process of providing patients with information needed to make voluntary health care decisions.
The first portion of the swing phase that includes early swing and acceleration.
Internal factors such as anatomy, age, biomechanics, or fitness level that predispose an individual to injury.
A reflex response to a trigger point palpation that includes a wincing or withdrawal reaction by the patient.
Grossly excessive scar formation.
The ability to perceive position and the quantity and speed of movement.
A by-product of muscle activity that leads to fatigue by reducing the muscle’s calcium-binding capacity and impairing glycogen breakdown.
A valgus traction force injury of the medial elbow that may start as an inflammatory response or apophysitis and progress to an avulsion of the apophysis if the repetitive stress continues.
Neurological findings indicating damage to motor pathways at or below the anterior horn cell. This may include flaccid paralysis, hypotonia, hyporeflexia, and muscle atrophy.
Avulsion of the extensor digitorum longus from the distal phalanx. Also known as baseball finger.

Connective-tissue cells. Also referred to as mastocytes and labrocytes. They store and produce various mediators of inflammation. Through their release of histamine, enzymes, and other mediators, mast cells cause increased local blood flow, attract immune cells, stimulate cell production of fibroblasts and endothelial cells, and promote and control remodeling of the extracellular matrix.
Sensory nerve endings that transmit light-touch sensation.
The portion of the stance phase of the gait cycle in which the foot is directly under the body’s weight and the entire foot is in contact with the floor. Also known as single-leg support.
The portion of the swing phase of the gait cycle in which the non-weight-bearing limb is in the middle of the swing phase.
Rare, chronic autoimmune disease marked by muscular weakness without atrophy.
The formation of bone within a muscle that has suffered a contusion.
Failure to act according to the standard of care, resulting in harm.
The ability to produce controlled movement through coordinated muscle activation.
A hormone that acts as a powerful vasoconstrictor at the immediate onset of injury. It may last from a few seconds to a few minutes.
A reflexive attempt to keep the eyes steady during body motion.
An inflammation or avulsion of the tibial apophysis, occurring in active, prepubescent children.
An inflammatory condition of shear stresses caused by either repeated trauma or strain on the symphysis pubis joint.
A condition caused by wearing and thinning of articular cartilage in a joint, eventually producing degeneration of the joint’s articular surface. Also known as degenerative joint disease (DJD).
Osteogenic cells from periosteum. They lay down the callus of fractured bone and convert later to chondrocytes.
Avascular necrosis of a joint’s articular surface.
Large multinuclear cells. Resorb dead, necrotic bone tissue.
A mild to moderate bone density loss that places the person at risk for developing osteoporosis.
A marked decrease in bone density causing bone porosity and brittleness with increased risk of fracture.

Afferent nerve endings that lie throughout the joint capsule and periarticular structures. They are rapidly adapting receptors thought to be compression sensitive, especially during high-velocity changes when the joint accelerates or decelerates as it moves into its limits of motion.
Position of the patella higher than normal in the patellofemoral groove.
Position of the patella lower than normal in the patellofemoral groove.
A foot with a high longitudinal arch.
A foot with a flat longitudinal arch.
Inflammation of the plantar fascia at its attachment to the calcaneus.

Subacromial impingement in the shoulder that occurs because of a mechanical narrowing of the subacromial space; tendinous, bursal, or bony structures within the space reduce the available area in the space.
Strategies intended to prevent injury or illness before it occurs through risk reduction and health promotion.
An immediate, systematic assessment focusing on airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure.
The angle formed by a line from the anterior superior iliac spine to the middle patella and a line from the middle patella to the tibial tubercle.
An abnormal condition in which the calcaneus is everted relative to the tibia.
An abnormal condition in which the calcaneus is inverted relative to the posterior bisection of the lower leg.
The time immediately following a stimulation when the muscle fiber is unable to respond to additional stimuli. It is divided into an absolute refractory period and a relative refractory period.

Impaired physiological functioning caused by relative energy deficiency; includes, but is not limited to, impairments of metabolic rate, menstrual function, bone health, immunity, protein synthesis, and cardiovascular health.
A test for balance in which a patient stands with feet together, and eyes closed. Increased postural sway compared to when eyes are open is a positive sign.

These afferent receptors are in the joint capsule on the flexion side of the joint. They slowly adapt and respond more to loads on the connective tissue in which they are contained than to the displacement of that connective tissue. These receptors are stimulated by extreme joint motion when the capsule is stressed in extension with rotation.
Calluses on the back of the hand or knuckles from self-induced vomiting.
Fracture involving the epiphyseal plate.
The activities and procedures a health care professional is legally permitted to perform.
Rapid and often fatal cerebral oedema resulting from a second concussion before recovery from a previous one.

The subacromial space narrows because of a dysfunction of the shoulder, so the humeral head is not centered as it should be within the glenoid. Muscle imbalances and posture leading to these imbalances often result in this impingement.
Strategies focused on early detection and intervention to limit the severity or duration of injury or illness.
A detailed head-to-toe evaluation conducted after life-threatening issues are addressed.
The ability of a clinical test to correctly identify individuals who have a condition.
Superior labrum tear, anterior and posterior in location. A tear in the labrum appears superiorly either on the posterior or anterior glenoid.
A fracture to the distal radius resulting from a fall on the outstretched hand with the wrist in a hyperflexed position.
The ability of a clinical test to correctly identify individuals who do not have a condition.
Patellae that are angled toward each other rather than facing forward.
The portion of the gait cycle during which the foot is in contact with the floor and the extremity is bearing partial or total body weight. In running, sometimes called the support phase.
The level of care expected of a reasonably competent professional under similar circumstances.
The distance from heel strike of one foot to heel strike of the same foot in one gait cycle.
In running, the inverse of cycle time or stride time: the number of step lengths over a given period.
The body’s side-to-side movement as weight is shifted from one lower extremity to the other.
A deformity caused by hyperextension at the PIP joint and hyperflexion at the DIP joint due to disruption of the volar plate and tensioning of the flexor tendons.
The time during which the foot is not in contact with the floor and no weight is borne on the extremity.
The middle of the swing phase. Also called mid-swing.
A three-point gait pattern with crutches in which the weight-bearing leg is advanced far enough to land in front of the crutches.
A three-point gait pattern with crutches in which the patient swings the weight-bearing leg to the crutches.

The passive insufficiency of the long finger tendons. When the wrist flexes, the long finger extensors extend, and when the wrist extends, the long finger flexors flex. This motion allows the opening and closing of the hand to be controlled passively by the wrist.
Strategies aimed at reducing long-term impairments, disability, or complications following injury or illness.
Intrinsic muscles of the thumb that include the abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, and adductor pollicis.
A clinical term that describes compression of the neurovascular structures as they exit through the thoracic outlet at the base of the neck.
An enzyme that converts fibrinogen to fibrin to form a fibrin plug early in the inflammation phase. In later inflammation, it stimulates fibronectin production and fibroblast proliferation.
An abnormal structural condition in which the tibia is rotated along its longitudinal axis so that the foot is rotated laterally beyond the normal 15° in relation to the patella’s midline.
An abnormal condition in which the distal tibia is closer to the midline than the proximal tibia.
The portion of the gait cycle during which the foot comes off the floor and the swing phase begins. Also called push-off or preswing.
Traumatic, unilateral, Bankart lesion, surgery required. A traumatic shoulder injury involving a tear of the anterior capsulolabral complex and requiring surgical repair.

Neurological findings indicating damage to motor pathways above the anterior horn cell. This may include spasticity, hyperreflexia, the presence of pathological reflexes, clonus, and a positive Babinski sign.
Alignment of a joint or stress to the joint that places the distal bone in a lateral direction; the “knock-kneed” position of the knee joint.
Alignment of a joint or stress to the joint that places the distal bone in a medial direction; the “bow-legged” position of the knee joint.
Transmitted by an animal (i.e., exposure to infected animals such as dogs, raccoons, insects, and bats).
A protein present in the blood that is important in initiating platelet adhesion to initiate the platelet plug formation.
Degeneration of a nerve fiber that occurs distal to the site of injury when the nerve fiber is cut or crushed.
A law that states that tissue, particularly bone, will adapt to the loads under which it is placed.
